Quote from: Tom Terrific on March 19, 2008, 11:15:19 AM a) which jobs don't qualify at CLS that would at HLS? the only thing H's websiTe mentions that isn't in Columbia's info packet is "academic". NGO, 501(c)3, Government work, and Clerkships seem to be covered in general. All jobs that are law related and make under the threshold amount qualify at HLS. There are no other requirements whatsoever. You could be practicing law that was aimed squarely against the public interest, and so long as you were making less than a certain amount, you'd still be covered. Harvard also covers non-law-related jobs, but only so long as they are in the categories you listed, I believe. The one job that is never covered is a clerkship, unless you intend to be covered by LIPP afterwards. If you do, then you are given a loan that will not be forgiven if you end up working for high levels of pay. Quote from: Tom Terrific on March 19, 2008, 11:15:19 AM b) According to the website, LIPP is also funded by loans unless you're self-employed, working for Harvard, or for a for-profit. The big difference is that HLS forgives each years worth of loans at the end of the year. CLS doesn't start forgiving loans until after your 3rd year, and you'd still have to repay at least some of the assistance if you quit your PI job during year 4 or year 5. You don't get to harvard levels of forgiveness until after 5 years. Loans of one year are, essentially, grants. They are just given in the form of forgivable loans to avoid tax liability. texts.full_text: she was eating lunch and reading the paper in the original looking 1920's art deco kitchen. The light was soft and I wanted to take her picture. I asked, and she said not in her cleaning clothes and kerchief. She wanted me to wait until she had her nice clothes on after her eight hours were up. So I did, but the picture was disappointing. All in all, she was treated very well, her hours were the same as mine, and like me when I worked shit jobs in high school and uni, she had ambitions for better work. So the whole maid thing no longer seemed like such a bad thing. I did get to see Happy again at this visit. She only works a couple of afternoons a week at Auntie J's because she's gotten a job in a doctor's office, and she's studying by correspondence to be a bookkeeper. Her hair hung in long beautiful braids, and (I hope this isn't rude but) she was much more gorgeous than the last time we saw her (and she was pretty the last time we saw her). It was really good to see her. When she got ready to leave shortly after we arrived, she got changed into her street clothes, which were totally high fashion, complete with broad diagonal stripes, big earring

; }; export default PollyComponent; ``` ---

texts.full_text: with limitations At my organization we want to have a central storage location where users can share their files. The problem is when they no longer needs these files they forget to delete them and then we end up having a problem of what we can and cant delete. So we are looking to see if there is a GPO or something that has it where it will automatically be deleted after a period or a way to enforce that the files get deleted when they are no longer in use. All of our servers are running 08 R2 SP2. A: First thing is first, you need to clearly define the criteria that you are using for this. What does "no longer in use" mean? Does that mean that the "Last Accessed" attrHere's how you can approach this problem: 1. Define Clear Criteria: Determine what "no longer in use" means for your organization. This could be based on the "Last Accessed" or "Last Modified" date. For example, you might decide that if a file hasn't been accessed in 90 days, it can be considered for deletion. 2. Implement File Screening: You can use PowerShell scripts or third-party tools to scan your central storage location and identify files that meet your criteria. Here's a simple PowerShell script that finds files not accessed in the last 90 days: ```powershell $path = "\\YourServer\Share" $days = 90 Get-ChildItem -Path $path -Recurse | Where-Object { $_.LastAccessTime -lt (Get-Date).AddDays(-$days) } ``` 3. Review and Approve: Before automatically deleting files, it's a good idea to have a review process. This could be as simple as emailing a list of files to the relevant department or user for approval. 4. Automate Deletion: Once approved, you can automate the deletion process. Again, PowerShell can help with this: ```powershell $path = "\\YourServer\Share" $days = 90 Get-ChildItem -Path $path -Recurse | Where-Object { $_.LastAccessTime -lt (Get-Date).AddDays(-$days) } | Remove-Item -Force ``` 5. texts.full_text: te changed its `HibernateException` (which was a checked exception) to an unchecked (`RuntimeException`). This change aligns more closely with modern best practices in handling persistence layer errors. Here are some key points: 1. Reducing Boilerplate Code: Checked exceptions require you to either catch them or declare their propagation, which can lead to excessive try-catch blocks and cluttered code that is harder to read and maintain. 2. Separation of Concerns: - Business logic should be concerned with business operations rather than the technicalities of database interactions. - By making persistence-related exceptions unchecked (runtime), Hibernate encourages developers to handle these issues at a higher level, typically in global exception handlers or application-level error management systems. 3. Flexibility and Modularity: Unchecked exceptions allow for more flexible code design where specific handling can be added later without affecting existing methods' signatures. 4. Error Handling Strategy: - The decision to use unchecked runtime exceptions reflects a shift towards treating data access issues as fatal application errors that should terminate the operation or, in some cases, cause an immediate response such as logging and notification. 5. Modern Java Practices: As of recent versions (Java 7 onwards), checked exceptions have been somewhat de-emphasized for certain scenarios where unchecked runtime exceptions are more appropriate due to their simplicity and effectiveness. This change was made after careful consideration by the Hibernate developers, balancing practicality with robust error handling strategies in modern application development. For further reading on this topic within the context of Java Persistence API (JPA) best practices, you might want to refer specifically to sections discussing exception managemen

texts.full_text: nd appearance in testifying, and the proper weight to be accorded to the evidence. While the solemn verdict of a jury should not lightly be vacated the responsibility nevertheless rests with the court again to review the cause and only after such review to decide the application for a new trial. (Olinger v. Pacific Greyhound Lines, 7 Cal.App.2d 484 [46 PaCal.2d 774].) If such review produces a conviction of insufficiency to support the verdict it is the plain duty of the trial court to set it aside and order a new trial. v. Bacos, 14 Cal.App.2d 338, 349 [58 PaCal.2d 221].) [2] The fact that the evidence submitted during the trial was wholly that of the prosecution and stands uncontroverted by evidence produced on behalf of the respondent can make no difference in the exercise of that supervisory power which the trial court possesses over the verdict of the jury. In such a case the same duty rests upon the judge of satisfying himself that guilt has been established that is imposed upon him in cases where the evidence is Even though all the evidence which was produced points in one direction, the court is not required to believe it. The trial judge has the same opportunity afforded to the jury of observing the manner of witnesses, the character of the testimony they give, and of discovering their motives and their interest and of judging their credibility. He should himself be satisfied that the evidence as a whole was sufficient to *730 sustain the verdict. If he is unable to arrive at this conclusion the granting of a new trial is not merely a proper exercise of legal discretion. It is a duty incumbent upon him by virtue of the office which he holds. (People v. Knutte, 111 Cal. 453 [44 P. 166]; People v. Canfield, 173 Cal. 309 [159 P. 1046]; People v. Ferlin, 203 Cal. 587 [265 P. 230];

Thirty three, 28 and 1.1% of strains isolated from hospitalized, outpatients and carriers, respectively, were methicillin-resistant. Six resistance patterns were found. texts.full_text: twenty-one common organic solvents (including ethanol, acetonitrile, dimethyl sulfoxide, dichloromethane, etc.) along with a polar protic solvent water and an apolar nonpolar solvent hexane were studied. The absorption of AG-1478 in these solvents was measured using UV-Vis spectrophotometer. AG-1478 is a selective inhibitor for c-Src kinase which has been shown to be critical in many human cancers such as breast, colon and cancer. The absorption spectrum of the tyrosine kinase inhibitor AG-1478 consists of two main bands at approximately 340nm (high energy band) and 330nm (low energy band). The energy absorption peak shifted from 327nm to 336nm with different solvents, indicating that this transition is sensitive to changes in dielectric constant or dipole moment of the surrounding environment. On the other hand, the lower energy absorption shift between 331nm and 334nm was less pronounced. To provide further insights into these phenomena, we calculated UV-Vis spectra using density functional theory (DFT) method for different solvents with varied polarity values ranging from highly polar to nonpolarity. These calculations were carried out at the B3LYP/6-31G(d,p) level of theory. Our theoretical study suggests that the observed changes in UV-Vis spectra are primarily attributed to alterations in solvent polarity and donor acceptor interactions within AG-1478 molecule, rather than its electronic transitions. Specifically, with an increase in solvent polarity from water (ε = 81) towards hexane (ε = 1), we observe a red-shift of the higher energy absorption band while the lower energy one remains almost unchanged. The UV-Vis spectroscopy results for AG-1478 are consistent across various polar and nonpolar organic solvents. However, the shift in peak positions was found to be on the polarity value (ε) as well as features of each solvent molecule such as hydrogen bond donor or acceptor properties. In conclusion, our study demonstrates that the UV-Vis spectroscopy is a powerful tool

texts.full_text: Search Search Legix.info Applying Legislative Technologies Category Archives: Akoma Ntoso Akoma Ntoso, Process Legislative Archeology January 13, 2022 grantcv1 Leave a comment One of the cool aspects of my job is that I get to work in different legislative traditions around the world. Sometimes it feels like a bit of an archeological dig, uncovering the history of how a jurisdiction came to be. Traditions are layered upon other traditions to result in unique practices that are clearly derived from one another. While I am no scholar in the subject and I’ve yet to come across any definitive description of the subject, I find exploring the subject quite fascinating. So far I’ve come across four distinct, but related, legislative traditions: The Westminster-inspired traditions found in the UK and around the world in the far reaches of the former...British Empire. These are characterized by a strong emphasis on parliamentary sovereignty, where power is vested primarily with Parliament. I've also encountered similarities between these systems and those influenced directly or indirectly from ancient Rome's system (specifically Lex Romana) which has left its mark in countries like France that adopted the Napoleonic Code as their primary legislative document. Another tradition I have come across involves a unique combination of elements inspired by Roman law, with influences taken heavily also into some US State Laws. Lastly there is what could be called 'common-law' or simply common - which has no single definition and can vary greatly between jur

texts.full_text: remaining northern one-third consists primarily of farmland interspersed with some residential areas and woodlands. The land in this part of Paris Township has been predominantly used for agricultural activities since the colonial period due to its fertile soil conditions suitable for farming crops such as corn, wheat, soybeans, and hay. Paris Creek runs through a portion of the township from northwestern to southeastern parts providing essential water resources both for agriculture and local wildlife. The creek is part of Ohio's watershed system feeding into larger rivers like the Cuyahoga River in Mahoning County before eventually reaching Lake Erie. The Paris Township government operates out of its own offices located on State Route 88, which serves as a main thoroughfare through township roads and connects to other significant routes such as Interstate 76 (Ohio Turnpike) providing regional connectivity. The local governmental structure includes trustees who oversee the operations within their jurisdiction. In terms of education facilities serving Paris Township residents are primarily handled by Portage County's school district, which offers several public schools including high, middle and elementary levels located in nearby municipalities like Ravenna or Garrettsville due to a lack of municipal-based educational institutions. The nearest post-secondary institution is Kent State University situated approximately 30 miles southwest. Paris Township has been known for its picturesque landscapes with rolling hills and lush forests that make it an attractive area both for outdoor enthusiasts who enjoy activities such as hiking, hunting or fishing in the numerous parks within township boundaries like Paris Creek Park. texts.full_text: 50 by soprano Kirsten Flagstad and the Philharmonia Orchestra, conducted by Wilhelm Furtwängler. The work has no opus number, and was published in 1950 after Strauss's death. It is listed as a part of his works "Die Alpen" (The Alps), although it was composed before that collection had been completed. "The Four Last Songs" are among the most emotionally powerful and musically sophisticated works of Strauss' later career, and they remain popular with audiences today. The songs reflect on themes such as nature's beauty, time passing, and mortality. The texts for "Frühling," "September," "Beim Schlafengehen," and "Im Abendrot" were written by various poets: - "Frühling": Friedrich Hölderlin (translated into English in the opera as "Spring"). - "September": Hermann Strohe. - "Beim Schlafengehen": Hugo von Hoffmannsthal, a friend and collaborator of Strauss' who wrote several other texts for his music. The title is an adaptation from Goethe's poem "Fälle." - "Im Abendrot": Christian Morgenstern. The works were published as a single volume in 1950 by the publishing house Südwestdeutscher Verlag in Karlsruhe, with cover art by Paul Klee. The publication included both Strauss's music and the poetic texts that inspired it. In addition to the original version for soprano and orchestra, the "Four Last Songs" have been transcribed

texts.full_text: the positive psychology of character strengths and virtues as well as social constructionist perspectives on psychotherapy. The contributions of the positive psychology of character strengths and social constructionist conceptualizations of psychotherapy are examined. In addition, the theoretical assumptions, applications, and limitations of Strength-Centered Therapy are discussed. It Strengths Centering is a therapeutic approach that combines elements from both traditional therapy models as well to newer perspectives within its field; specifically drawing upon concepts like character strengths or virtues alongside social constructionist ideas about how people construct meaning through language/communication processes during sessions together with clients who present various issues related either directly (e.g., depression) indirectly(anxiety). The foundation lies primarily around understanding one's personal traits - including those considered positive ones such as courage, honesty wisdom etc.- while also considering their contextual factors that shape behavior according to social constructionist theory; this allows therapists employing SCT techniques access into exploring deeper layers beneath surface level problems experienced by individuals seeking help from them. This therapy can be applied across various contexts and populations with its focus not solely being limited towards addressing mental health concerns but extending further so it may enhance well-being overall through building upon these identified strengths instead focusing exclusively on weaknesses or deficits present within an individual's life experiences which could lead him/her down negative paths otherwise avoided if proper attention were given earlier stages developmentally speaking. However, there are some limitations to Strengths Centered Therapy as with any therapeutic approach; it may not be suitable for all clients and their specific needs especially those suffering from severe mental health issues requiring more traditional forms treatment like cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT). Additionally this type might require significant time commitment due its comprehensive nature which could pose challenges when trying implement such practices into already busy schedules both professionally

texts.full_text: re setters at runtime. It can be less ugly with lambda functions. You can devise clever ways to populate this map. This is the only alternative I know to avoid reflection. I'm doing this in a discrete event simulator that I'm writing since reflections is slow compared to this approach. Also, with reflection you cannot obfuscate your code. public class PojoTest { public int a; public int b; public int c; private Map<String, Setter> setters = new HashMap<String, Setter>(); public PojoTest() { initSetters(); } public void set(Map<String, Integer> map) { for (String s : map.keySet()) { setField(s, map.get(s)); } } public String toString() { return a + ", " + b + ", " + c; } public static void main(String[] args) { PojoTest t = new PojoTest(); Map<String, Integer> m = new HashMap<>(); m.put("a", 1); m.put("b", 2); m.put("c", 3); t.set(m); System.out.println(t); } private void setField(String s, Object o) { setters.get(s).set(o); } private void initSetters() { setters.put("a", new Setter() { @Override public void set(Object o) { a = (Integer) o; } }); setters.put("b", new Setter() { @Override public void set(Object o) { b = (Integer) o; } }); setters.put("c", new Setter() { @Override public void set(Object o) { c = (Integer) o; } }); } priva

texts.full_text: ive and negative symptoms: historical and conceptual aspects. The re-emergence of interest in negative symptoms during the past decade has generated a creative period for the study of psychopathology in schizophrenia. Increasingly, a relative balance has been restored to descriptive psychopathology, giving more equal emphasis on florid colorful symptoms such as delusions and hallucinations as well as the more debilitating negative symptoms. An emphasis on considering the neural mechanisms that may underlie both positive and negative symptoms has led to a more focused approach to asking questions about psychopathology, particularly with regard o schizophrenic disorders. texts.full_text: begins with the ink being delivered under pressure or by other means from an external cartridge through a supply needle to the recording head. This liquid, now termed 'ink', flows into the internal channels of the jet head where it is subjected to precise manipulation using piezoelectric elements or thermal energy sources that convert electrical signals into mechanical forces causing rapid volumetric change in ink chambers adjacent to nozzles. In this environment, any air bubbles present within the liquid can disrupt the normal operation by occupying space intended for ink droplets. The continuous flow of bubble-free ink is thus vital for maintaining print quality and preventing ejection failures like missing dots or inconsistent drop sizes that degrade image resolution on printed media such as paper. To ensure an uninterrupted supply, a filter component is strategically placed in the conduit between the needle connection point to where it meets with internal channels of the ink jet head. This placement helps trap air bubbles before they can enter and potentially disrupt ejection performance while also preventing foreign particles from reaching nozzles that could cause blockages or damage. In summary, this text outlines a critical aspect in maintaining efficient operation of an ink-jet recording system by addressing how it mitigates the risks associated with entrained air within liquid supply. Through such measures as incorporating filters into its design at strategic points along the flow path from storage to ejection mechanism, the device ensures that only bubble-free and clean ink reaches the nozzles for printing purposes. Such an ink jet recording head has a problem that air bubbles contained in ink or air bubbles entrained in ink during attachment or removal of the liquid storing unit may enter the ink jet recording head, leading to an ejection failure such as missing dots.
